offence: Contravention of Personal Data Protection Principles
description: Failure to comply with any of the seven personal data protection principles under Part II Division 1 (sections 6-12), including the General Principle, Notice and Choice Principle, Disclosure Principle, Security Principle, Retention Principle, Data Integrity Principle, and Access Principle
compoundRegulation: Personal Data Protection (Compounding of Offences) Regulations 2016
notes: This is the primary offence under the PDPA. Each principle violation is a separate offence. The 2024 Amendment significantly increased penalties to strengthen deterrence.

offence: Processing Personal Data Without Registration
description: Processing personal data as a data controller belonging to a class of data controllers required to register under the Act, without being registered or without a valid certificate of registration
compoundRegulation: Personal Data Protection (Compounding of Offences) Regulations 2016
notes: Applies only to data controllers in a prescribed class under the Class of Data Users Order 2013. Non-registered classes are subject to the Act's principles but not the registration requirement.

offence: Failure to Register as Data Controller
description: Failure by a data controller in a prescribed class to apply for registration with the Commissioner before processing personal data, or continuing to process personal data after a registration is revoked or suspended
compoundRegulation: Personal Data Protection (Compounding of Offences) Regulations 2016
notes: Related to but distinct from s5(2). The registration obligation arises at the point a data controller begins operations; s5(2) covers the ongoing processing without registration.

offence: Non-Compliance with Cross-Border Personal Data Transfer Requirements
description: Transfer or permitting the transfer of personal data to a place outside Malaysia without complying with the prescribed requirements, including transfers to countries not approved by the Minister and failure to implement adequate safeguards where required
compoundRegulation: Personal Data Protection (Compounding of Offences) Regulations 2016
notes: Section 129A was substantially revised by A1727. The 2024 amendment introduced new mechanisms including binding corporate rules, standard contractual clauses, and certification schemes as alternatives to ministerial approval.

offence: Unauthorized Disclosure by Commissioner or Officers
description: Disclosure by the Commissioner, Deputy Commissioner, officer or employee of the Department, or any person acting on behalf of or assisting the Commissioner, of any information that comes to their knowledge in the course of performing their duties, otherwise than in accordance with the Act
notes: This provision protects the confidentiality of information obtained during enforcement and regulatory activities. It applies to current and former officers and their agents. Section reference corrected from s130 (which, post-A1727, is 'Liability of body corporate') to s57 ('Secrecy') where the secrecy obligation and offence for Commissioner and officers resides in the post-A1727 provisions database.

offence: Obstruction of Commissioner or Authorized Officers
description: Wilfully obstructing the Commissioner or any authorized officer in the exercise of their powers or the performance of their duties, including refusing to produce documents, providing false information, or hindering inspections and investigations
notes: Obstruction includes failure to cooperate with audits, inspections, and investigations. The enhanced penalty post-2024 reflects the increased importance of effective enforcement mechanisms. Section reference corrected from s131 (which, post-A1727, is 'Service of notices') to s116 ('Obstruction of enforcement officers') where this offence resides in the post-A1727 provisions database.

offence: Unauthorized Disclosure by Data Controller
description: Disclosure of personal data by a data controller, data processor, or their employees in contravention of the Act, including disclosure for unauthorized purposes, to unauthorized parties, or beyond the scope for which the data was collected and consented to
compoundRegulation: Personal Data Protection (Compounding of Offences) Regulations 2016
notes: Distinct from the Disclosure Principle (s8) violation. This provision targets deliberate unauthorized disclosures by persons who have access to personal data in the course of their employment or engagement.
note: POST-A1727 DISCREPANCY: In the post-A1727 provisions database, s132 is 'Evidence' (a procedural provision), not an offence for unauthorized disclosure. The pre-amendment unauthorized disclosure offence has likely been consolidated into s129 ('Offences in relation to personal data') post-A1727. This section reference (s132) reflects the pre-amendment numbering and must be verified against the gazetted amended Act text.

offence: Failure to Comply with Enforcement Notice
description: Failure to comply with any requirement set out in an enforcement notice issued by the Commissioner, including notices requiring the data controller to take specific remedial action, cease processing, or comply with any provision of the Act within a specified timeframe
notes: An enforcement notice is a formal regulatory direction. Non-compliance undermines the entire enforcement regime. This offence can be charged in addition to the underlying breach that triggered the notice. Section reference corrected from s133 (which, post-A1727, is 'Presumptions') to s29 ('Failure to comply with enforcement notice') where this offence resides in the post-A1727 provisions database.

offence: Officer Personal Liability for Body Corporate Offences
description: Where a body corporate commits an offence under the Act, any director, chief executive officer, chief operating officer, manager, secretary or other similar officer, or any person purporting to act in that capacity, who consented to, connived in, or whose neglect contributed to the commission of the offence, is also guilty of the offence
notes: This is personal liability of directors and senior officers — distinct from the body corporate's own liability under s130 (turnover penalty). Officers cannot shelter behind corporate structures where they consented to, connived in, or whose neglect contributed to PDPA violations. Introduced by A1727.

offence: False Statements and Misleading Information
description: Making any statement or furnishing any document or information to the Commissioner or any authorized officer that is false or misleading in a material particular, whether in the context of registration, audit, inspection, investigation, or any other proceeding under the Act
notes: Covers both written and oral statements. Applies whether the false statement was made knowingly or recklessly. Applies to data controllers, data subjects, data processors, and any other person providing information under the Act.
note: POST-A1727 DISCREPANCY: In the post-A1727 provisions database, s134 is 'Public servants' (an administrative provision), not an offence for false statements. The pre-amendment false statements offence has likely been renumbered due to the insertion of new sections by A1727. This section reference (s134) reflects the pre-amendment numbering and must be verified against the gazetted amended Act text.

offence: Unauthorized Sale of Personal Data
description: Selling, offering to sell, or procuring for sale any personal data in contravention of the Act, including bulk sales of personal data obtained without the consent of the data subjects, or selling data in breach of the purpose limitation or disclosure restrictions
notes: New offence inserted by the 2024 Amendment Act in response to growing concern over the commercial trade in personal data. Targets both the seller and any person who procures or facilitates the sale.

offence: Failure to Notify Data Breach
description: Failure by a data controller to notify the Commissioner of a notifiable data breach as soon as practicable, or failure to notify affected data subjects as soon as practicable where required
compoundRegulation: Personal Data Protection (Compounding of Offences) Regulations 2016
notes: New obligation inserted by A1727. Section 143A(5) creates the offence for failure to notify. Continuing failure is a continuing offence. Note: The JPDP Guideline on Data Breach Notification 2025 (effective 1 June 2025) specifies 72 hours for initial notification to JPDP and 7 days for data subject notification — these are guideline timelines, not statutory requirements under the Act itself.

offence: Failure to Appoint Data Protection Officer
description: Failure by a data controller who is required under the Act and guidelines to appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO) to do so, or failure to register the appointed DPO with the Commissioner within the prescribed period, or appointing a person who does not meet the prescribed qualifications
compoundRegulation: Personal Data Protection (Compounding of Offences) Regulations 2016
notes: New obligation introduced by A1727. DPO appointment is mandatory for data controllers processing large volumes of data or processing sensitive personal data. The DPO Guideline 2025 sets out criteria for mandatory appointment.

offence: Contravention of Data Subject Rights
description: Failure by a data controller to comply with an access request, correction request, or opt-out request by a data subject within the prescribed time, or charging fees in excess of the prescribed amount, or refusing an access request without lawful grounds
compoundRegulation: Personal Data Protection (Compounding of Offences) Regulations 2016
notes: Covers access requests (s30), correction requests (s34), and opt-out from direct marketing (s43). Each unanswered or improperly answered request may constitute a separate offence.
note: POST-A1727 DISCREPANCY: In the post-A1727 provisions database, s44 is 'Right to restrict processing' (a new rights-based provision inserted by A1727 s18), not a penalty/offence section. The pre-amendment offence for contravention of data subject rights has been renumbered due to insertion of ss41-44 (new data subject rights). The penalty for non-compliance with data subject rights may now fall under s128 ('General offences'). This section reference (s44) reflects the pre-amendment numbering.

offence: Processing Sensitive Personal Data Without Explicit Consent
description: Processing sensitive personal data including health data, political opinions, religious beliefs, commission or alleged commission of offences, and other prescribed categories without obtaining the explicit consent of the data subject or without falling within the narrow statutory exemptions
compoundRegulation: Personal Data Protection (Compounding of Offences) Regulations 2016
notes: Sensitive personal data attracts a higher standard of protection. The Act does not require an additional lawful basis beyond explicit consent except in very limited circumstances (e.g., medical emergencies, legal proceedings). Section reference corrected from s29 (which, post-A1727, is 'Failure to comply with enforcement notice') to s17 ('Processing of sensitive personal data') where this obligation and its offence reside in the post-A1727 provisions database.

offence: General Penalty
description: Where no specific penalty is provided elsewhere in the Act for a contravention of any provision, the general penalty applies. This is a catch-all provision for technical or administrative breaches not covered by more specific penalty sections
compoundRegulation: Personal Data Protection (Compounding of Offences) Regulations 2016
notes: The 2024 Amendment elevated the general penalty and added an imprisonment option where none existed before, reflecting the legislature's intent to treat PDPA violations as serious offences.

offence: Continuing Offence
description: Where a person is convicted of an offence under the Act and the contravention continues after the conviction, that person commits a further offence for each day or part of a day during which the contravention continues, rendering the person liable to a daily fine in addition to the original penalty
notes: The continuing offence provision is critical for compelling compliance. Where a data controller refuses or is slow to remedy a violation, the accumulating daily fine creates strong financial incentive to comply promptly.
note: If Act A1727 renumbered the original Continuing Offence provision to s139 or similar, the section reference in this entry should be updated accordingly. Verify against the gazetted amended Act text.

offence: Body Corporate Contravention — Turnover-Based Penalty
description: Where a body corporate commits an offence under the Act, the body corporate is liable to a penalty calculated on the basis of annual Malaysian turnover. This is the primary corporate liability provision and applies in addition to any fine imposed on individual officers under s133A.

penalty.fine: Greater of RM3,000,000 or 10% of annual gross turnover in Malaysia

amendedPenalty.fine: Greater of RM3,000,000 or 10% of annual gross turnover in Malaysia
amendedPenalty.notes: Turnover-based penalty — the RM3M floor ensures a minimum sanction even for smaller organisations

notes: Inserted by A1727. Applies to body corporates only. Individual officers may additionally be liable under s133A. The 10% annual Malaysian turnover cap applies only to Malaysian-derived revenue, not global turnover.

offence: Failure to Conduct Data Protection Impact Assessment
description: Failure by a data controller to conduct a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) before undertaking high-risk processing activities as required under s137. High-risk processing includes large-scale processing of sensitive personal data, systematic monitoring of publicly accessible areas, and automated decision-making with significant effects on data subjects.
compoundRegulation: Personal Data Protection (Compounding of Offences) Regulations 2016
notes: New obligation inserted by A1727. High-risk processing includes large-scale processing of sensitive data, systematic monitoring, and automated decision-making with significant effects on data subjects. 18-month transition period applies.

offence: Failure to Maintain Records of Processing Activities (ROPA)
description: Failure by a data controller to maintain Records of Processing Activities as required for prescribed classes of data controllers. ROPA must record identity and contact details of the data controller, DPO contact, purposes of processing, categories of data subjects and data, recipients, cross-border transfers, retention periods, and security measures.
compoundRegulation: Personal Data Protection (Compounding of Offences) Regulations 2016
notes: ROPA must record: identity and contact details of data controller, DPO contact, purposes of processing, categories of data subjects and data, recipients, cross-border transfers, retention periods, and security measures.

offence: Failure to Cooperate with Investigation
description: Failure to comply with a requirement under Part VIII (Inspection, Complaint and Investigation), including failure to produce documents or records requested by the Commissioner, failure to attend for examination, or failure to answer questions put by an authorized officer in the course of an investigation
notes: Applies to both data controllers and third parties who may hold relevant information. Privilege against self-incrimination does not apply to the production of documents in administrative proceedings under the Act.
note: POST-A1727 DISCREPANCY: In the post-A1727 provisions database, s109 is 'Confidentiality of complaints' (an administrative obligation), not an offence for failure to cooperate. The offence for failing to attend/answer questions under investigation is at s104 ('Power to require attendance') in the post-A1727 database. This section reference (s109) reflects the pre-amendment numbering and should likely be updated to s104.

offence: Failure to Comply with Production Order
description: Failure by any person to comply with a production order issued by a Magistrate requiring the production of documents, data, records, or other material relating to an offence under the Act
notes: A production order is a compulsory document production mechanism akin to a legal subpoena. Non-compliance is treated seriously as it frustrates the enforcement mandate of the Commissioner. Section reference corrected from s127 (which, post-A1727, is 'Public prosecutor') to s114 ('Production order') where the production order offence resides in the post-A1727 provisions database.
